text:
Pre-trained language models (PLMs) in recent years have been developing at a very fast pace. And it is widely accepted that larger models lead to better performance. However, as the models grow in size, fine-tuning all parameters of the model becomes too expensive and not practical due to the large amount of computational resources as well as storage space required. Hence, parameter efficient few-shot fine-tuning of large pre-trained language models has become a new trend where only a few percent of the total number of parameters are updated by fine-tuning, while the majority of the weights are kept unchanged.

Recent advances in pre-trained language models (PLMs) have made parameter adaptation a ubiquitous technique in natural language processing (NLP). However, these approaches are scattered and appear to be ad hoc. In this paper, we unify these approaches by naming them delta-tuning (denoted as $\delta$), which refers to the set of changes (parameters to be updated and their corresponding updates) in the PLM. We formally define the optimization problem and present a taxonomy to classify all the delta-tuning based methods. We analyze the theoretical roots of the success of delta-tuning from the perspectives of optimization theory and optimal control. Experimental results are provided to support our theoretical analysis by evaluating the performance of various delta-tuning based methods on 133 NLP tasks. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of delta-tuning based methods in terms of efficiency, stability and model performance.
